_note: Evergreen F1 trivia pool. Hand-curated from web research (Wikipedia, formula1.com, StatsF1, Motorsport.com) and verified for accuracy. Facts are deliberately settled history, not live standings, so they never go stale. Shown on the home page dot-matrix trivia board. category: milestone | record | quirky | tech.

facts.text: The first F1 World Championship race was the 1950 British Grand Prix at Silverstone on 13 May 1950.
Giuseppe "Nino" Farina won that first race and became F1's first World Champion in 1950 with Alfa Romeo.
Juan Manuel Fangio was F1's first five-time World Champion, a record that stood for 46 years.
Fangio won his five titles with four different teams: Alfa Romeo, Maserati, Mercedes and Ferrari.
Ferrari took its first F1 win at the 1951 British Grand Prix, Froilan Gonzalez ending Alfa Romeo's streak.
The 1953 Argentine Grand Prix was the first F1 World Championship race held outside Europe.
Mercedes scored its first works F1 win on debut at the 1954 French Grand Prix with Fangio.
Stirling Moss became the first British driver to win an F1 World Championship race at the 1955 British GP.
Maria Teresa de Filippis became the first woman to start an F1 World Championship race, in 1958.
Vanwall won the first ever F1 Constructors' Championship in 1958, the year the title was introduced.
Mike Hawthorn became the first British F1 World Champion in 1958, beating Moss by a single point.
The Cooper T43 was the first rear-engined car to win an F1 race, at the 1958 Argentine GP.
Jack Brabham's 1959 title made the rear-engined Cooper the first champion car with its engine behind the driver.
The first United States Grand Prix in the World Championship was held at Sebring in December 1959.
McLaren scored its first F1 win at the 1968 Belgian GP, Bruce McLaren winning in a car bearing his name.
The first Japanese Grand Prix was held at Fuji in 1976, deciding the Hunt-Lauda title fight.
The Renault RS01 brought F1's first regularly-raced turbo engine when it debuted in 1977.
Renault took the first turbocharged F1 win at the 1979 French GP, Jabouille leading an all-French effort.
Williams won its first F1 race at the 1979 British GP, Clay Regazzoni taking victory at Silverstone.
Michael Schumacher became the first German F1 World Champion in 1994.
Lewis Hamilton was the first Black driver in F1, debuting with McLaren in 2007.
Hamilton took a podium on his F1 debut in 2007, the first rookie to do so since Villeneuve in 1996.
The 2008 Singapore Grand Prix was F1's first night race, run under floodlights at Marina Bay.
Fangio won the very first World Championship Monaco Grand Prix in 1950 for Alfa Romeo.
The 1950 Monaco Grand Prix was also Ferrari's first ever race in Formula One.
Sebastian Vettel is F1's youngest world champion, crowned in 2010 aged just 23 years and 134 days.
Juan Manuel Fangio is the oldest F1 world champion, taking his fifth title in 1957 aged 46.
Max Verstappen is F1's youngest race winner, taking the 2016 Spanish GP aged 18 years and 228 days.
Luigi Fagioli remains F1's oldest race winner, sharing victory at the 1951 French GP aged 53.
Niki Lauda won the 1984 title by half a point over Alain Prost, the closest championship finish ever.
The half point that decided the 1984 title came from the rain-shortened Monaco Grand Prix.
Lewis Hamilton snatched the 2008 title by one point, passing Glock on the final corner of the final lap.
Felipe Massa led the 2008 title for about 30 seconds before Hamilton's last-corner pass settled it.
Fangio's 24 wins from 51 starts give him the highest win rate in F1 history, roughly 47%.
Michael Schumacher won five drivers' titles in a row from 2000 to 2004, a record run.
Kimi Raikkonen ended the longest winless wait in F1 history, 114 races, at the 2018 United States GP.
Riccardo Patrese's 6.5-year gap between his 1983 and 1990 wins is the longest by time between victories.
Alberto Ascari won nine consecutive championship Grands Prix across the 1952 and 1953 seasons.
Brawn GP won both world titles in 2009, the only season the team ever competed.
Jenson Button won his sole F1 title in 2009, Brawn GP's one and only season.
Pastor Maldonado's 2012 Spanish GP win was his only F1 victory and Venezuela's first.
No driver has won more titles with one team than Lewis Hamilton's six championships at Mercedes.
Michael Schumacher set a record 13 race wins in a single season in 2004, from 18 rounds.
Michael Schumacher and Lewis Hamilton share the record for most drivers' titles, with seven each.
Sebastian Vettel won four straight F1 titles from 2010 to 2013 with Red Bull.
Nigel Mansell was F1's oldest modern-era race winner, taking victory at 41 in 1994.
Lella Lombardi is the only woman to score in F1: half a point at the shortened 1975 Spanish Grand Prix.
Thai royal Prince Bira raced in early F1 under the pseudonym "B. Bira", his chosen nom de course.
At Monaco in 1950 Fangio dodged a hidden pileup after noticing the crowd was facing away from him.
The shortest F1 race ever was the 1991 Australian GP: 14 laps, stopped by rain after just 24 minutes.
John Watson won the 1983 US GP West from 22nd on the grid, the lowest starting spot ever to win a race.
Monaco's famous swimming pool section appeared in 1973 when the track was rerouted around a new pool.
Nigel Mansell collapsed trying to push his Lotus over the line in 40C heat at the 1984 Dallas GP.
Niki Lauda was nicknamed "The Rat" for his buck teeth, and later upgraded himself to "King Rat".
James Hunt won the 1976 British GP after a near-riot got him reinstated, only to be disqualified later.
The longest track ever to host an F1 race was Italy's 25.6 km Pescara circuit, used just once in 1957.
Berlin's AVUS hosted one 1959 F1 race on a 43-degree banked wall, the steepest ever used in racing.
Giancarlo Baghetti is the only driver to win his first World Championship race, the 1961 French GP.
The Indianapolis 500 counted toward the F1 World Championship from 1950 to 1960, though few F1 regulars ran it.
Andrea Moda, run by a shoe magnate, was so chaotic it was banned mid-1992 for bringing F1 into disrepute.
F1's closest finish: Peter Gethin beat Ronnie Peterson by 0.01s at Monza in 1971.
Stirling Moss, runner-up four years running, is the greatest driver never to win the F1 World Championship.
At 1982 Imola, Pironi snatched victory from teammate Villeneuve, who vowed never to speak to him again.
The Hills and Rosbergs are the only father-son pairs to each win an F1 World Championship.
Graham Hill is the only driver ever to win the Triple Crown: Monaco GP, Indy 500 and Le Mans 24 Hours.
The six-wheeled Tyrrell P34, with four small front tyres, is the only six-wheeler to win an F1 race, in 1976.
Brabham's "fan car" won its only F1 race in 1978, then was withdrawn, leaving a perfect 100% win record.
Juan Manuel Fangio was kidnapped by Castro's rebels before the 1958 Cuban GP and released a day later, unharmed.
Hans Heyer sneaked into the 1977 German GP unqualified, the only man with a DNQ, DNF and DSQ in one race.
The 1950 British GP is the only F1 race attended by a reigning British monarch, King George VI.
Spanish marquess Alfonso de Portago juggled F1, the Olympic bobsleigh and riding in the Grand National.
Kimi Raikkonen, "The Iceman", calmly ate an ice cream in the cockpit during a red flag at the 2009 Malaysian GP.
Andrea de Cesaris started 208 F1 races without ever winning one, long the record for futility.
Louis Chiron is the oldest man to start an F1 race, lining up at Monaco in 1955 aged 55.
Max Verstappen debuted in 2015 aged 17, prompting the FIA to set a minimum F1 age of 18.
Jack Brabham ran out of fuel on the last lap of the 1959 US GP and pushed his car over the line to take the title.
Bruce McLaren won the 1959 US GP aged 22, a youngest-winner record that stood for 44 years.
Jim Clark won the 1965 F1 title and the Indy 500 the same year, skipping Monaco to race at the Brickyard.
Mike Hawthorn, Britain's first F1 champion in 1958, raced wearing a bow tie and retired straight after winning.
At the 2005 US GP only six cars raced after all Michelin-shod teams withdrew over tyre safety fears.
The old Nurburgring Nordschleife, used by F1 until 1976, was 22.8 km, the longest circuit ever to host a GP.
Niki Lauda's 1976 crash on the Nordschleife ended F1's use of the fearsome 22.8 km circuit for good.
The original Spa-Francorchamps was a 14.1 km road circuit; F1 last raced its full layout in 1970.
Spa's Eau Rouge-Raidillon is a steep uphill left-right-left taken at nearly 300 km/h by modern F1 cars.
Suzuka's 130R corner is named for its 130-metre radius and is taken near 320 km/h.
Monaco's Fairmont Hairpin is the slowest corner in F1, taken at roughly 50 km/h.
Monza is the fastest circuit on the F1 calendar, with average lap speeds around 260 km/h.
The 1.5-litre supercharged BRM V16 of the early 1950s revved to nearly 12,000 rpm and made around 600 bhp.
In 1986 BMW's 1.5-litre turbo four made over 1,300 bhp in qualifying trim, the most powerful F1 engine ever.
F1's first aerodynamic wings appeared in 1968, with Lotus, Ferrari and Brabham all adopting them that year.
Tall strut-mounted wings were banned after 1969 when several collapsed at speed, causing dangerous crashes.
The Lotus 79 of 1978 perfected ground effect, using underbody venturi tunnels and skirts to grip the track.
Sliding skirts and ground effect were outlawed for 1983 by mandatory flat-bottom rules after a spate of crashes.
The 1981 McLaren MP4/1 was the first F1 car to race with a carbon-fibre monocoque, designed by John Barnard.
From 2006 F1 switched to 2.4-litre naturally aspirated V8 engines, replacing the screaming 3-litre V10s.
The 2006 Cosworth V8 reportedly revved past 20,000 rpm before rev limits were brought in to cap engines.
Since 2014, F1 has used 1.6-litre V6 turbo-hybrid power units combining a combustion engine with energy recovery.
KERS, a kinetic energy recovery system, debuted in F1 in 2009 to harvest and redeploy braking energy.
DRS, the moveable rear-wing Drag Reduction System, was introduced in 2011 to aid overtaking.
In-race refuelling was banned in 1984, returned in 1994, then banned again from 2010 on cost and safety grounds.
Peter Gethin won the 1971 Italian GP at an average of 242.6 km/h, the fastest F1 race for over 30 years.
Juan Pablo Montoya hit about 372 km/h at Monza in 2005, long cited as the fastest speed at an F1 weekend.
In 2006 a modified BAR-Honda set an F1 land-speed record of 397.36 km/h at the Bonneville Salt Flats.
Indianapolis, which hosted the F1-counting Indy 500 from 1950 to 1960, is nicknamed the Brickyard.
Monaco is the slowest circuit on the F1 calendar, with pole laps averaging under 170 km/h.
Monza's Parabolica is a long, fast right-hander that sweeps cars onto the main straight at high speed.
